sanskrit:
यत्रोपरमते चित्तं निरुद्धं योगसेवया।
यत्र चैवात्मनाऽऽत्मानं पश्यन्नात्मनि तुष्यति॥
सुखमात्यन्तिकं यत्तद्बुद्धिग्राह्यमतीन्द्रियम्।
वेत्ति यत्र न चैवायं स्थितश्चलति तत्त्वतः॥
transliteration:
yatroparamate cittaṃ niruddhaṃ yoga-sevayā |
yatra caivātmanātmānaṃ paśyann ātmani tuṣyati ||
sukham ātyantikaṃ yat tad buddhi-grāhyam atīndriyam |
vetti yatra na caivāyaṃ sthitaś calati tattvataḥ ||
literal_meaning: Where the mind is restrained by yoga-practice and ceases (its wanderings), where the self beholds the self by the pure mind and is satisfied in the self, where one knows that supreme happiness which is grasped by the intellect alone, beyond the senses, and being thus established does not swerve from the truth—
contextual_meaning: Krishna describes the moment when sustained meditative effort culminates in a self-validating, affect-laden insight: the mind’s turbulence subsides, the observing self recognizes itself as separate from mental content, and a non-sensory joy arises that is so convincing it becomes an internal refuge immune to external loss or pain.
psychological_state: Post-traumatic growth meets flow: the nervous system shifts from threat-scanning to safe-connection, the default-mode network quiets, and the medial prefrontal cortex integrates limbic input into a coherent narrative of self-as-awareness rather than self-as-story.
trigger_scenario: After months of daily mindfulness practice, a war-veteran therapist notices that the intrusive memory of a buddy’s death no longer hijacks her body; she feels an unexpected wave of warmth in her chest, sees her reflection in the mirror, and quietly knows, 'I am not my trauma.'
anchor_text: Still the mind, meet your self, and rest in wordless joy.
